Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon, which wasn't the best approach. Some weeks I'd put in decent hours, maybe spread across a few days, but other weeks I'd barely touch anything. I'm enrolled in nine online courses, so keeping track of everything felt overwhelming and I just kind of drifted through it.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, really. Deadlines were the main thing that got me moving—if something was due, I'd cram. Otherwise, I'd find myself doing other things, like my extracurricular activities or just zoning out. There was no set schedule; it depended entirely on what felt urgent at the moment.
Q3: Usually I'd log in because I remembered an assignment was coming up or because someone in my peer group mentioned something about the course. Sometimes I'd get a notification and think, 'Oh right, I should check that.' It wasn't like a habit where I'd just naturally open it every day—it was more reactive, you know?
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my day. There were brief periods where I'd check in more regularly, especially when peer support sessions reminded me to stay on track. But those moments didn't last long, and I'd slip back into only logging in when absolutely necessary.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful for me since I learn better through text. I'd download PDFs and go through them at my own pace. Discussion posts were occasionally helpful too, especially when classmates explained things in simpler terms. The videos were fine but I didn't always have the patience to sit through them.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the optional stuff and even some required videos. Without reliable internet at home, streaming long videos was a pain, so I'd skip them or only watch parts. Some discussion threads felt pointless—like people were just posting to meet a quota—so I didn't engage much there either.
Q7: If something directly helped me understand what I needed for an assignment or exam, it felt worth it. Anything that seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grade, I'd mentally file away as 'maybe later' and then forget about it. I needed to see immediate relevance.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt like a trap. Like, I knew it might help, but with nine courses and my anxiety about keeping up, I couldn't justify spending time on things that weren't mandatory. Required activities at least had a deadline pushing me, so I'd do those. Optional ones just fell off my radar.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download readings and notes, then study from those on my laptop without being online. I also used my own handwritten notes and sometimes external YouTube videos when I needed a different explanation. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reviewing my downloaded materials or rewriting notes by hand, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd spend hours going over stuff I'd saved, but to the system, it probably looked like I wasn't doing anything. My offline study was invisible.
Q11: All the time. I'd open a PDF once, download it, and then never access it through the LMS again even though I'd read it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides—grabbed them once and studied from my local copies. It was just easier without worrying about connectivity.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when assignments from all my courses piled up at once. My anxiety would spike and I'd feel paralyzed, like I couldn't even start anything. During those times, I'd avoid the LMS because just seeing all the tasks stressed me out more.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload was brutal with nine courses, and I'd get mentally exhausted. When I was fatigued or confused about something, I'd just close the laptop instead of pushing through. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars also meant the LMS wasn't always my priority.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd technically be 'engaged' according to the system, but I wasn't absorbing anything. It was more like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't sure how to start. The anxiety made it worse—I'd know something was important but the thought of tackling it made me freeze. I'd tell myself I'd do it later and then scramble at the last minute.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without a deadline looming, I probably wouldn't engage at all. As due dates approached, I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS, downloading everything, checking requirements, submitting work. It was a cycle of avoidance and panic.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but honestly, I didn't always check it promptly. When I did see positive feedback, it felt nice but didn't really change my habits. Negative feedback sometimes made my anxiety worse, which ironically made me want to engage less, not more.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty standard, but with so many courses, I couldn't really invest deeply in any single one. The weekly modules helped me know what was expected, but I often fell behind and then felt too overwhelmed to catch up properly. Structure helped, but only if I actually followed it.
Q19: Having clear deadlines and downloadable materials made things easier for me. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks spread out—it felt like death by a thousand cuts. Also, anything requiring consistent internet access was tough given my situation at home.
Q20: I think that could be someone exploring or trying to figure out what's expected without actually committing to the work. Maybe they're anxious about starting, like me, so they look at everything but can't bring themselves to produce anything. Or they're just lost and clicking around hoping something clicks.
Q21: That sounds like me, actually. I didn't log in much, but I studied offline quite a bit and managed to pass my exams decently. The LMS activity doesn't capture everything—someone might be grinding away with downloaded materials or textbooks and still do okay without showing up in the data.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you were technically online, but they miss the quality of that time. They can't tell if you were actually focused or just had a tab open. They also miss all the offline work, the thinking, the handwritten notes—basically the invisible learning.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and want to learn, not just survive. Honestly, I wasn't very engaged this semester—I was mostly just trying to get through it without failing, which isn't the same thing.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety affected my participation, or how I'd spend hours studying offline after downloading materials once. It wouldn't capture the peer support conversations that actually helped me stay somewhat on track, or the mental exhaustion from juggling nine courses. The human side of my experience is completely invisible.
